section: Disorders of the Heart Muscles Valves and Pericardium

items.subtopic: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Indications for Cardiac Transplantation in Advanced Heart Failure
items.content: Patients with advanced heart failure who are refractory to conventional therapy, exhibit poor cardiac reserve, and have a high risk of death may require surgical options like cardiac transplantation. This is due to their inability to tolerate neurohormonal antagonists at recommended doses and worsening renal function.
keyPoints: Advanced heart failure characterized by refractoriness to conventional therapy
Frequent hospitalization, poor quality of life, high risk of death
Inability to tolerate neurohormonal antagonists at recommended doses
Worsening renal function and poor cardiac reserve on cardiopulmonary stress testing
Low cardiac output state with elevated pulmonary pressures on invasive hemodynamic assessment
>5 decades since the first human-to-human heart transplant in 1967
1-year survival >80%, median survival of 12.5 years, conditional survival of 14.8 years if recipient survives the first year
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Criteria for Listing Candidates for Cardiac Transplantation
items.content: Due to the limited availability of organ donors, careful evaluation and listing of candidates for cardiac transplantation are crucial. The International Society for Heart and Lung Transplantation updated criteria in 2016 to include patients with congenital heart disease, restrictive and infiltrative cardiomyopathy, and chronic infections.
keyPoints: Demand for cardiac transplantation exceeds availability of organ donors
Optimal utility, equitable allocation, and patient autonomy are key in listing decisions

Criteria updated in 2016 to include congenital heart disease, restrictive and infiltrative cardiomyopathy (e.g., amyloidosis), and chronic infections (e.g., Chagas' disease, tuberculosis, viral hepatitis)
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION
items.title: Urgency and Allocation Criteria for Donor Hearts
items.content: The allocation of donor hearts prioritizes recipients based on clinical urgency, waiting time, geographical proximity, and blood group compatibility. Patients in critical status requiring advanced support like ECMO or mechanical circulatory devices are given the highest priority.
keyPoints: Urgency is determined by need for extracorporeal membrane oxygenation (ECMO) or temporary mechanical circulatory support.
Geographical allocation considers a 4-hour ischemia time limit unless novel transport techniques are used.
Blood group compatibility and size match are key features in the allocation offer.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION

items.subtopic: PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION
items.title: Donor-Recipient Size Matching and Age Considerations
items.content: The donor heart must be appropriately sized relative to the recipient, with specific weight thresholds depending on gender. Donor age is typically under 55 years but may exceed this due to organ shortages.
keyPoints: Size match criteria: donor body weight no greater than 30% below recipient for same sex, or 20% for female to male transplants.
Donor age typically restricted to <55 years, often exceeded in practice.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION

items.subtopic: PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION
items.title: Utilization of Donors After Circulatory Death and Hepatitis C Positive Organs
items.content: Donor hearts from circulatory death are increasingly used with comparable outcomes to brain death donors. Additionally, organs from hepatitis C positive donors are utilized due to effective post-transplant antiviral therapies.
keyPoints: Outcomes of donor hearts after circulatory death are similar to those from brain death donors.
Hepatitis C positive donor organs are used with subsequent antiviral therapy for recipients.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION

items.subtopic: SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION
items.title: Understanding Bicaval Orthotopic Cardiac Transplantation and Its Implications on Postoperative Management
items.content: The bicaval orthotopic cardiac transplant is the standard procedure that connects donor and recipient superior and inferior venae cavae, as well as aortic and pulmonary great vessels. This method retains the function of the donor right atrium, which is crucial for early postoperative right heart output and may prevent atrial arrhythmias. Due to surgical denervation, the allograft does not respond to physiological autonomic stimuli, necessitating high-dose catecholamines or drugs like isoproterenol for managing bradycardia.
keyPoints: Bicaval orthotopic cardiac transplant connects donor and recipient superior and inferior venae cavae, as well as aortic and pulmonary great vessels.
The technique maintains donor right atrium function, important for early postoperative right heart output.
Allograft is not responsive to physiologic sympathetic or parasympathetic stimuli due to surgical denervation.
Bradycardia cannot be treated with atropine; instead, use isoproterenol or temporary electrical pacing.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

items.subtopic: SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION
items.title: Standard Triple-Drug Immunosuppression Regimen for Cardiac Transplant Patients
items.content: The standard triple-drug immunosuppression regimen includes a calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporine or tacrolimus), corticosteroids, and antiproliferative agents like mycophenolate mofetil. The most commonly used combination for optimal outcomes is tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, and prednisone. In high-risk patients or those with renal dysfunction delaying calcineurin inhibitors, induction therapy using monoclonal or polyclonal antibodies may be employed.
keyPoints: Triple-drug immunosuppression includes a calcineurin inhibitor (cyclosporine or tacrolimus), corticosteroids, and antiproliferative agents.
Standard combination for best outcomes: tacrolimus, mycophenolate mofetil, prednisone.
Induction therapy with monoclonal or polyclonal antibodies is used in high-risk patients or when delaying calcineurin inhibitors.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

items.subtopic: SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION
items.title: Differentiating Acute Cellular Rejection (ACR) and Antibody-Mediated Rejection (AMR) in Cardiac Allografts
items.content: Acute cellular rejection (ACR) is a T cell-mediated process occurring early post-transplant, characterized by lymphocytic infiltrates. It typically declines after 6 months but can lead to hemodynamic compromise if severe. Treatment involves high-dose corticosteroids or rescue polyclonal antibodies in severe cases. Antibody-mediated rejection (AMR) is driven by circulating donor-specific antibodies causing immunoglobulin deposition and complement fixation, leading to allograft dysfunction and increased risk of cardiac allograft vasculopathy. AMR treatment focuses on antibody suppression using plasmapheresis, rituximab, bortezomib, or eculizumab.
keyPoints: ACR is T cell-mediated with lymphocytic infiltrates, declining after 6 months but can cause hemodynamic compromise if severe.
Treatment for ACR includes high-dose corticosteroids or rescue polyclonal antibodies in severe cases.
AMR involves circulating donor-specific antibodies causing immunoglobulin deposition and complement fixation.
AMR treatment uses plasmapheresis, rituximab, bortezomib, or eculizumab.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

items.subtopic: LATE COMPLICATIONS AFTER CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Understanding Cardiac Allograft Vasculopathy (CAV) and Its Management
items.content: Cardiac allograft vasculopathy (CAV) is a significant late complication following cardiac transplantation, characterized by proliferative thickening of the vascular intima. It results from chronic immunosuppression, ischemia-reperfusion injury, and early immunologic insults. CAV often develops silently, making early diagnosis crucial. Intravascular ultrasound or optical coherence tomography are more sensitive diagnostic tools than routine coronary angiography. The International Society for Heart and Lung Transplantation provides a grading scheme (CAV0 to CAV3) based on angiographic disease extent and allograft dysfunction. Statins and other lipid-lowering agents, along with antihypertensive medications and anti-CMV therapy, show potential in preventing or reducing CAV progression.
keyPoints: CAV is characterized by proliferative thickening of the vascular intima.
Intravascular ultrasound or optical coherence tomography are more sensitive than routine coronary angiography for diagnosing CAV.
Grading scheme: CAV0 (absent), CAV1 (mild), CAV2 (moderate), CAV3 (severe).
Angiographic CAV is present in ~30% of patients by 5 years and ~50% by 10 years post-transplant.
Statins may help prevent CAV; nonstatin lipid-lowering drugs like PCSK9 inhibitors show promise.
Antihypertensive agents and anti-CMV therapy have shown benefits in reducing CAV.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · LATE COMPLICATIONS AFTER CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: LATE COMPLICATIONS AFTER CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION
items.title: Malignancy Risks Post-Cardiac Transplantation: Prevention and Management
items.content: Post-cardiac transplantation patients face an increased risk of malignancies, particularly posttransplant lymphoproliferative disorders driven by Epstein-Barr virus. Immunosuppression is a key factor in the development of these malignancies. Management includes reducing immunosuppression, using targeted antiviral agents, and traditional chemo- and radiotherapy. Specific antilymphocyte therapy targeting CD20 has shown promise. Skin cancers are common, necessitating regular sunscreen use. While decreasing immunosuppression is a strategy for managing these cancers, its impact remains unclear, highlighting the need for future research on immune modulation and malignancy prevention.
keyPoints: Increased risk of malignancies post-cardiac transplantation due to immunosuppression.
Posttransplant lymphoproliferative disorders are most common, often driven by Epstein-Barr virus.
Management includes reducing immunosuppression, antiviral agents, and traditional therapies.
Specific antilymphocyte therapy targeting CD20 shows promise.
Skin cancers (basal cell and squamous cell carcinomas) are prevalent; regular sunscreen use is advised.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · LATE COMPLICATIONS AFTER CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.title: HeartMate 3 LVAS: Improved Outcomes in Heart Failure Patients
items.content: The HeartMate 3 LVAS, a centrifugal continuous flow pump with a fully magnetically levitated system, has demonstrated significant improvements in patient outcomes compared to previous devices. It offers reduced rates of adverse events such as stroke and bleeding complications.
keyPoints: HeartMate 3 LVAS reduces the need for pump replacement compared to HeartMate II.
Superior survival free from disabling stroke or reoperation with HeartMate 3.
5-year survival rate is nearly 60%, rivaling cardiac transplantation in younger, healthier patients (75% at 5 years).
Reduction in complications like pump thrombosis and stroke rates.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.title: Indications for LVAS Implantation in Heart Failure Patients
items.content: LVAS, particularly the HeartMate 3, is indicated for patients with severe persistent systolic heart failure who have not responded to optimal medical management. These patients often exhibit significant functional limitations and a poor prognosis without intervention.
keyPoints: Indicated for patients with peak oxygen consumption <12 mL/kg per min or those requiring continuous intravenous inotropic therapy.
Median survival without LVAS is often less than 1 year.
HeartMate 3 can substantially prolong life expectancy.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.title: Evolution of LVAS: From Pulsatile to Continuous Flow Systems
items.content: The transition from pulsatile flow devices, like those used in the REMATCH trial, to continuous flow systems such as HeartMate 2 and HeartMate 3 has led to improved durability, reduced complications, and better patient outcomes.
keyPoints: REMATCH trial showed a 48% reduction in risk of death with early-generation pulsatile LVAS.
Continuous flow systems have greater durability and fewer complications like strokes and infections.
HeartMate 3's design reduces hemolysis and improves long-term survival.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Optimal Blood Pressure Management for Continuous Flow LVAS
items.content: Continuous flow left ventricular assist devices (LVAS) depend on maintaining appropriate systemic blood pressure to ensure forward flow. Doppler ultrasound is used to measure mean arterial pressure due to the low pulsatile nature of blood flow in these devices.
keyPoints: Mean arterial blood pressure should be kept ≤ 90 mmHg.
Antihypertensive therapy often involves renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system drugs or other vasodilators.
Doppler ultrasound is used to measure mean or opening blood pressure.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Hemocompatibility and Neurologic Events in LVAS Patients
items.content: Patients with continuous flow LVAS are at risk for neurologic events such as ischemic and hemorrhagic strokes, often occurring during the surgical implant course. The ARIES HM3 study demonstrated that eliminating aspirin from antithrombotic regimens reduces nonsurgical bleeding.
keyPoints: Neurologic events can occur during surgical implant or later in patients with advanced heart failure.
Elimination of aspirin reduces risk of nonsurgical bleeding, decreasing hospitalizations and care costs.
Antithrombotic regimen includes a vitamin K antagonist targeting an international normalized ratio of 2–3.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.title: Infection Management in LVAS Patients
items.content: Infections, particularly involving the driveline, are common post-LVAS implantation. These infections necessitate long-term suppressive antibiotics and can lead to thrombosis and increased neurologic complications.
keyPoints: Driveline infections occur in 20–50% of patients at 5 years post-implant.
Treatment includes local internal exploration and long-term suppressive antibiotics.
Infections increase the risk of thrombosis and neurologic complications.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

Presence of concomitant pathology such as coronary artery disease

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of the 'bicaval' orthotopic cardiac transplant technique?
options: The donor left atrium is sutured to the recipient's right atrial tissue.
The recipient retains its own superior and inferior venae cavae.
The donor and recipient superior and inferior venae cavae are connected.
The allograft becomes responsive to physiologic sympathetic stimuli.
items.explanation: In the bicaval orthotopic cardiac transplant, the donor and recipient superior and inferior venae cavae are connected.

items.question: What is the treatment of choice for bradycardia in a cardiac allograft post-transplant?
items.explanation: Due to surgical denervation, atropine is ineffective for bradycardia in a cardiac allograft; isoproterenol or temporary electrical pacing is used instead.

items.statement: The HeartMate II is the most commonly used LVAS pump today.
items.explanation: The HeartMate 3 LVAS has replaced the HeartMate II as the most used pump.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.statement: The REMATCH trial demonstrated a median survival extension of 5 months with LVAS.
items.explanation: The REMATCH trial showed a median out-of-hospital survival prolongation by only 5 months.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Pump thrombosis is a common complication with the HeartMate 3 LVAS.
items.explanation: Pump thrombosis has been largely eliminated with the introduction of the HeartMate 3 LVAS.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.statement: Hemolysis can be indicated by elevated lactate dehydrogenase levels in patients with LVAD.
items.explanation: Lactate dehydrogenase is an excellent biomarker of hemolysis.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION
items.assertion: Advances in immunosuppression have contributed to improved outcomes in cardiac transplantation.
items.reason: These advances have helped manage allograft rejection and late complications such as accelerated coronary artery disease, malignancy, and renal failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why advances in immunosuppression have improved outcomes.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation | CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CANDIDATES FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION

items.subtopic: PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION
items.assertion: Donors after circulatory death can be evaluated as candidates for cardiac reanimation.
items.reason: Such donor organs obtained after circulatory death are gaining widespread acceptance with outcomes similar to those from brain death donors.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION

items.subtopic: PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION
items.assertion: The geographical regional reach for allocation is based solely on territorial considerations.
items.reason: The time that a donor heart would be in transit and therefore in out-of-body ischemia time is also considered.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because the passage states that both territorial considerations and ischemia time are factors.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PRINCIPLES OF DONOR RECOVERY AND ALLOCATION

items.subtopic: SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION
items.assertion: The cardiac allograft is not responsive to physiologic sympathetic or parasympathetic stimuli.
items.reason: This occurs due to surgical denervation during transplantation.
items.explanation: Surgical denervation results in the cardiac allograft being unresponsive to physiological autonomic stimuli.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

items.subtopic: SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION
items.assertion: Intravenous immunoglobulin is used as an immune-modulatory agent in the treatment of AMR.
items.reason: It helps suppress and remove circulating antibodies involved in AMR.
items.explanation: While intravenous immunoglobulin is used, its primary role is not to directly suppress or remove antibodies but as an immune-modulatory agent.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · SURGERY FOR CARDIAC TRANSPLANTATION | CARDIAC ALLOGRAFT REJECTION AND IMMUNOSUPPRESSION

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.assertion: The HeartMate 3 LVAS has improved long-term survival.
items.reason: It nearly eliminates pump thrombosis and markedly reduces stroke rates.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reduction in adverse events explaining the improved survival.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS
items.assertion: The HeartMate II is still widely used.
items.reason: It demonstrated a 48% reduction in risk of death in the REMATCH trial.
items.explanation: The assertion is false as the HeartMate 2 is no longer widely used, though the reason about its past performance is true.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · PROLONGED ASSISTED CIRCULATION | LEFT VENTRICULAR ASSIST SYSTEMS AND CLINICAL TRIALS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: The unsupported right ventricle often demonstrates worsening function.
items.reason: Increased device flow results in a greater right heart preload and reduced right ventricular contractile efficiency.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
items.assertion: Strokes in patients with HeartMate 3 LVAS occur only during surgical implant.
items.reason: Later occurrences of strokes are similar to those observed in advanced heart failure patients not supported by LVAS.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; strokes can also occur later, not just during the surgical implant.
items.reference: Cardiac Transplantation and Prolonged Assisted Circulation · MANAGEMENT OF LVAS AND THEIR COMPLICATIONS
